title: New Locally Decodable Codes from Lifting
speaker: Madhu Sudan
abstract: Locally decodable codes (LDCs) are error-correcting codes that allow for highly-efficient recovery of "pieces" of information even after arbitrary corruption of a codeword. Locally testable codes (LTCs) are those that allow for highly-efficient testing to see if some given word is close to a codeword. Codes derived from evaluations of low-degree multivariate polynomials give the simplest

proof_stages.label: Local-code objectives and parameters
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes finite-field linear-code notation, rate and distance, and formalizes local testing and local correction as sublinear oracle algorithms with locality L and error parameter epsilon.

proof_stages.label: Reed--Muller line-locality baseline
proof_stages.summary: Reed--Muller codes are reviewed as evaluations of low-degree multivariate polynomials whose restrictions to random affine lines give q-query local correction and testing, but with a rate barrier such as at most one half in the first nontrivial case.

proof_stages.label: Known projection dimensions for Reed--Muller decoding and testing
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys prior Reed--Muller decoding and low-degree-testing results, explaining how projection dimension is dictated by the first dimension where nontrivial degree constraints appear.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-driven testing obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A finite-field Frobenius degree drop shows that over non-prime fields line restrictions can hide high total degree, making the classical testing dimension larger and motivating the later reversal from testing polynomials to defining codes by the test itself.

proof_stages.label: Landscape and lifted-code target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Existing local-testable and local-decodable code constructions, including multiplicity codes and weak lower bounds, are used to isolate the new goal: lifted codes of rate tending to one and locality n^epsilon that are both locally correctable and locally testable.

proof_stages.label: Affine-invariant lifting and inherited locality
proof_stages.summary: The lifted code is defined by requiring every affine t-dimensional restriction to lie in an affine-invariant base code, after which distance, local correction, and local testing are inherited essentially generically from the base code, leaving rate as the main quantity to analyze.

proof_stages.label: High-rate monomial and carry analysis
proof_stages.summary: For line-lifts of high-degree univariate codes over small-characteristic fields, Lucas-theorem bit conditions and a carry-avoidance probability argument show that almost all monomial exponent vectors are admitted, so the lifted code has rate tending to one while retaining n^epsilon locality.

proof_stages.label: Nikodym-set application and affine-invariant program
proof_stages.summary: The large dimension and low-degree line restrictions of lifted codes replace ordinary low-degree polynomials in a polynomial-method proof for finite-field Nikodym sets, and the talk closes by positioning affine-invariant codes as a broader source of local-code constructions.

examples.name: Frobenius degree-drop monomial over a non-prime field
examples.note: The monomial x^{q/2}y^{q/2} over characteristic two restricts to degree q/2 on every line, exposing why non-prime-field low-degree tests need larger testing dimension and suggesting the flipped viewpoint behind lifted codes.

examples.name: Raz--Safra two-dimensional low-degree test
examples.note: The two-dimensional projection test is cited as the rare known case where using more queries than the minimal line test gives a cleaner or stronger analysis, illustrating the discussion of testing dimension.
